raw_case:
Undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma at the anastomosis after ileocecal resection for colon cancer: A case report


Introduction

Spindle cell sarcoma (SCS) is a sarcoma subtype rarely described in the abdominal cavity, and with a worse prognosis compared with that at other sites. We report a case of SCS occurring at the anastomosis after ileal resection for colorectal cancer.

Presentation of case

An 86-year-old woman with a chief complaint of abdominal pain had undergone ileal resection, D2 dissection, and hand-sewn anastomosis in 2011 to treat papillary stage I adenocarcinoma. In February 2023, the patient was referred to our hospital because of progressive anemia, and a full circumferential type 3 tumor was found at the anastomosis site after resection of the ascending colon. Biopsy revealed a malignant spindle tumor. The patient underwent an open right hemicolectomy of the colon and limited lymph node dissection. The final pathological examination revealed an undifferentiated SCS centered on the anastomosis. Postoperative follow-up imaging is ongoing, and there has been no recurrence.

Discussion

SCSs are a group of tumors composed of spindle-shaped cells. Diagnosis based solely on morphology is difficult. The final diagnosis was based on the results of immunostaining and genetic testing. A diagnosis of sarcomatous transformation due to local recurrence was ruled out. Since the possibility of a dedifferentiated liposarcoma remained, a definitive diagnosis of undifferentiated SCS with an undetermined differentiation direction was not confirmed.

Conclusion

SCS is a rare disease, and this is the first reported case of SCS occurring in the colon or during colonic anastomosis. Additional cases are necessary to determine an appropriate treatment strategy.

Highlights

•Spindle cell sarcomas occurring in the intestinal tract are rare.•We report a case at the anastomosis after ileal resection for colorectal cancer.•The final diagnosis was based on the results of immunostaining and genetic testing.1

Introduction

Primary spindle cell sarcoma (SCS) is a subtype of mesenchymal sarcoma, a broad term for cancers that arise in connective tissues. Primary mesenchymal sarcomas of the gastrointestinal system are rare, comprising just 0.1 %–3 % of all gastrointestinal tumors [1].SCSs can occur in almost any region [2], but are more common in superficial sites such as the head, neck, and limbs [3]. Middle-aged and older adults (50–70 years) are most commonly affected, and they are more frequent in men [4]. Abdominal occurrence (133 in 3299 cases) has been reported to be an indicator of poor prognosis [2].Various treatment options have been proposed, including chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and surgery. Surgical resection achieves a better prognosis than non-resection [3], and surgical therapy remains the gold standard for soft tissue sarcomas. However, the importance of lymph node dissection and extensive resection remains controversial.Here we describe a case of SCS originating at the anastomosis site after ileocecal resection for ascending colon cancer. SCSs occurring in the intestinal tract are rare. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of an SCS occurring at an anastomosis site after colon resection for colorectal cancer. The work has been reported in line with the SCARE criteria [5].2

Presentation of case

An 86-year-old woman presented to our hospital with a chief complaint of epigastric pain. She had undergone ileocecal resection for ascending colon cancer 10 years previously (pT2N0M0, pStageI; TNM classification 8th ed.), and no recurrence was reported. In June 2022, she visited her local doctor who referred her to our hospital after encapsulated fluid retention and scattered small nodules were found in the abdominal cavity on computed tomography (Fig. 1A,B).2.1

Differential diagnosis, investigations, and treatment

The primary lesion could not be identified, but it resolved spontaneously after approximately 6 months. The encapsulated fluid retention disappeared and no clinical cause could be identified. In February 2023, the patient was referred to our hospital for progressive anemia. A circumferential type 2 tumor was found at the anastomosis after resection of the ascending colon by colonoscopy, which had not been found in July 2022 (Fig. 2). The tumor was located at the ascending colon, and a biopsy revealed a malignant spindle tumor. Therefore, the patient was referred to our department. Blood tests showed no elevation of tumor markers, and the results of other tests were normal. Computed tomography revealed irregular wall thickening of 25 mm in the ascending colon and a 37-mm tumor in the mesentery of the small intestine, without other obvious lesions (Fig. 3A,B). Magnetic resonance imaging showed a pale high signal on T2-weighted and diffusion-weighted images, as well as a high signal on fat-suppressed T1-weighted images, suggesting mucus accumulation (Fig. 4A,B).The patient was diagnosed with a malignant spindle cell tumor localized at the anastomosis. In March 2023, the patient underwent open right hemicolectomy of the colon and limited lymph node dissection (owing to previous dissection during the original surgery). Only one small nodule was found intraoperatively in the abdominal cavity, which was submitted for pathological examination and diagnosed as scar tissue. The operative time was 252 min, with a blood loss of 120 mL. The patient was discharged on postoperative day 8 without any complications. Macroscopically, the anastomosis around the ascending colon, which was the primary site, revealed a circumferential type 3 tumor sized 84 × 52 mm with necrotic tissue adhered to the superficial layer (Fig. 5A). Histologically, the tumor was grayish-white, dense, and situated in the intrinsic muscularis propria, with atypical spindle-shaped cells showing atypical nuclear fission (Fig. 5B). The colon serosa exhibited fibrous hyperplasia, vessel hyperplasia, inflammatory cell infiltration, and necrotic tissue (Fig. 5C). Immunohistochemical analysis showed that the tumor cells were positive for α1-antichymotrypsin (Fig. 5D), vimentin (Fig. 5E), MDM2, and CDK4, and negative for S-100- and HMB-45. Thus, the possibility of dedifferentiated liposarcoma could not be ruled out. Pathological examination revealed an undifferentiated SCS centered on the anastomosis. The patient recovered well. After 1 year, computed tomography and colonoscopy did not reveal any signs of local recurrence or distant metastasis. Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for the publication of this report and accompanying images.3

Discussion

Sarcomas are malignant mesenchymal tumors subdivided into osteosarcomas and soft-tissue sarcomas. Gastrointestinal sarcomas occur more commonly in the small intestine, stomach, and esophagus of older adults [6]. Common clinical manifestations have been reported to be abdominal pain, abdominal mass, and fever, in that order [7,8]. Undifferentiated sarcoma, a soft-tissue sarcoma first described and named by Ozzello et al. [9], is a high-grade disease with a reported median survival of <6 months [10]. SCS is a type of undifferentiated sarcoma, characterized by the presence of spindle cells and usually originating from the extremities. Although SCSs have been reported to occur intraperitoneally, there are no previous reports of SCSs occurring intracolonally or at the anastomotic site, as was the case here.As part of the natural response to soft tissue injury, spindle cells present in the injured tissue begin to divide to promote healing. Normally, these cells stop replicating as the affected area heals. However, for reasons not fully understood, they may continue to divide uncontrollably. The cells in excess may then accumulate and combine to form an SCS. Risk factors include Paget's disease of bone, radiation therapy for bone infarction or cancer, and osteomyelitis. We hypothesized that chronic inflammation of the isthmus may have contributed to the observed outcome.SCSs belong to a group of tumors that are morphologically composed of spindle-shaped cells. Making a diagnosis based on morphology alone is difficult, and a final diagnosis is based on immunostaining and genetic testing. In the present case, the tumor developed at the anastomotic site, and sarcomatoid changes of carcinoma were first considered, but this possibility was ruled out because there was no clear evidence of carcinoma, and the tissue was negative for AE1/AE3. The other immunostaining results showed high malignancy and suggested a high-grade SCS. Although the tumor originated within the intestinal wall, gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) and leiomyosarcoma were also ruled out owing to differences in histology. Synovial sarcoma and clear cell sarcoma were also ruled out, leading to the diagnosis of sarcoma with an unknown differentiation direction.In contrast, cells were positive for MDM2 and CDK4, suggesting the possibility of a dedifferentiated component in dedifferentiated liposarcoma (DLPS). In such cases, a well-differentiated liposarcoma (WLPS) component is often present in the surrounding areas. In the present case, no WLPS component was found in the excised specimen. There was a preoperative history of fluid retention and spontaneous disappearance of the nodule. Although spontaneous resolution of WLPS has not been previously reported, this is difficult to assess in practice. Only one small nodule was found intraoperatively in the abdominal cavity and was submitted to pathology. However, the diagnosis was scar tissue, and no WLPS component was detected in the resection specimen. Therefore, although the possibility of a DLPS diagnosis is low in this case, careful follow-up will continue because this condition is rare and no clear treatment guidelines have been established.To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of SCS occurring in the colon. The frequent recurrence of undifferentiated soft-tissue sarcoma is problematic. Fortunately, the tumor did not show distant metastasis and could be completely resected surgically. However, undifferentiated sarcomas metastasize early, and once distant metastasis develops, it cannot be radically removed by surgery, and the prognosis is very poor. The 2-year overall survival rate for anaplastic sarcoma has been reported to be approximately 60 %. The generally short follow-up period for patients with anaplastic sarcoma, with some patients missing follow-up data and no available reports of survival rates beyond 5 years, indicates that the overall prognosis for patients with anaplastic pleomorphic sarcoma of the colon may be even worse [7]. Local recurrence is also common in soft-tissue sarcomas, within a reported median period of 19 [11] and 15.7 months [12]. Thus, frequent follow-up is recommended after sarcoma resection, especially during the first 2 years [13]. Although complete surgical resection is the mainstay of treatment, there are no fixed standards for lymph node dissection, partly owing to the lack of reports. In the present case, although lymph node dissection was limited to D1 dissection, complete resection was achieved grossly and image-wise, and a certain prognosis was expected. Adjuvant radiation therapy and chemotherapy may be tried postoperatively, depending on the condition and intraoperative status of the patient, but further research is needed to select a specific regimen because of individual differences in efficacy. Adriamycin and ifosfamide should be considered in the treatment of soft-tissue sarcomas if there is suspicion of recurrence. Molecular biology studies of undifferentiated sarcoma of the colon may provide the basis for targeted therapies and immunotherapy, and offer hope for an improvement in the prognosis of these patients.4

Conclusion

SCS is a rare disease, and this is the first reported case occurring in the colon or during colonic anastomosis. The treatment strategy remains controversial, and additional cases are needed to determine the optimal approach.

Author contribution

NK and MK conceived the presented idea, developed the theory, and performed the computations. MT, MN, TA, MI, RH and KT encouraged the investigation of specific aspects and supervised the findings of this study. All authors discussed the results and contributed to the final manuscript.

Consent to participate

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient to publish this case report with the accompanying images.

Ethics approval

This study was approved by the ethics committee of our institution and conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki.

Guarantor

Nao Kitasaki

Funding

This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Conflict of interest statement

None.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 86-year-old woman.
- **Chief Complaint:** Abdominal pain and progressive anemia.
- **History of Present Illness:** The patient underwent ileocecal resection 10 years prior for stage I ascending colon adenocarcinoma. Subsequent encapsulated fluid retention and scattered nodules resolved spontaneously in June 2022. In February 2023, colonoscopy detected a circumferential type 2 tumor at the anastomosis site. Biopsy showed a malignant spindle cell tumor.
- **Past Medical History:** Ileocecal resection for colon cancer (pT2N0M0, pStage I).
- **Personal History:** None.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** No specific findings reported beyond the intraoperative discovery of a single small abdominal nodule (later diagnosed as scar tissue).
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Colonoscopy findings: Circumferential lesion at the anastomosis site, biopsy revealed malignant spindle tumor.
  - Blood tests: No tumor marker elevation.
  - Imaging: CT demonstrated thickened ascending colon wall (25 mm) and 37-mm mesenteric tumor. MRI findings were suggestive of mucus accumulation. Immunohistochemistry showed tumor positivity for α1-antichymotrypsin, vimentin, MDM2, and CDK4, and negativity for AE1/AE3, S-100, and HMB-45.
  - Histology: Dense tumor in the muscularis propria with atypical spindle-shaped cells. Final pathology showed undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma (SCS).
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:**
   - Malignancy at the anastomosis site prompted a differential diagnosis including recurrent carcinoma, gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST), leiomyosarcoma, synovial sarcoma, clear cell sarcoma, and liposarcoma.
   
2. **Exclusions:**
   - **Sarcomatoid carcinoma:** Ruled out by absence of carcinoma evidence and AE1/AE3 negativity.
   - **GIST and leiomyosarcoma:** Excluded based on histological differences and immunostaining results.
   - **Synovial sarcoma and clear cell sarcoma:** Excluded based on histology.
   - **Dedifferentiated liposarcoma (DLPS):** Excluded due to lack of well-differentiated liposarcoma components despite MDM2 and CDK4 positivity.

3. **Final Considerations:**
   - Diagnosis of undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma was made based on morphology, immunostaining, and exclusion of other entities.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma (SCS) at the anastomosis site following prior ileocecal resection for colorectal cancer.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case step by step. First, the patient is an 86-year-old woman with a history of colon cancer treated with ileocecal resection 10 years ago. Now, she's presenting with abdominal pain and progressive anemia. 

The key points here are the prior colon cancer (stage I, pT2N0M0) and the recent findings. The colonoscopy found a circumferential tumor at the anastomosis site. The biopsy shows a malignant spindle cell tumor. Spindle cells can be seen in various tumors, like sarcomas, gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST), or even some carcinomas with spindle cell differentiation. But the immunohistochemistry results might help here.

Looking at the IHC: positive for α1-antichymotrypsin, vimentin, MDM2, and CDK4. Negative for AE1/AE3 (which are cytokeratin markers, so that might rule out carcinoma), S-100 (which would be positive in melanoma or nerve sheath tumors), and HMB-45 (also melanoma-related). MDM2 and CDK4 positivity often points towards well-differentiated liposarcoma or dedifferentiated liposarcoma, but those are more common in retroperitoneum or extremities. However, MDM2 amplification is a marker for certain sarcomas. 

The histology mentions dense tumor in the muscularis propria with atypical spindle cells, leading to a diagnosis of undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma. But wait, the original colon cancer was adenocarcinoma. Now, a spindle cell tumor at the anastomotic site. Could this be a recurrence of the colon cancer but with sarcomatoid differentiation? Or a secondary primary tumor? 

But the biopsy says malignant spindle cell tumor, and the IHC is negative for epithelial markers (AE1/AE3), which suggests it's not a carcinoma. So more likely a sarcoma. The MDM2 and CDK4 positivity might suggest a sarcoma with amplification of these genes. However, undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma (SCS) is a diagnosis of exclusion when other specific markers are absent. But in this case, MDM2 and CDK4 are positive. MDM2 and CDK4 co-expression is typically seen in well-differentiated/dedifferentiated liposarcoma. But the location here is the colon mesentery. However, dedifferentiated liposarcoma can occur in the abdomen. But the tumor is at the anastomotic site, which is in the colon. 

Wait, the imaging showed a mesenteric tumor as well. So the primary could be in the mesentery. But the colonoscopy found a tumor at the anastomosis. The pathology report says the final diagnosis is undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma. However, MDM2 and CDK4 positivity might suggest a different type. But perhaps in this case, the sarcoma is a dedifferentiated liposarcoma arising in the mesentery, but the colon involvement is secondary. But the initial colon cancer was stage I, and now a new tumor at the anastomosis site. The fact that the spindle cell tumor is at the site of previous surgery might also raise the possibility of a radiation-associated sarcoma if she had radiation, but there's no mention of that. Alternatively, a secondary sarcoma arising in the scar tissue. 

Alternatively, could this be a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST)? But GISTs are usually positive for CD117 (c-KIT) and DOG1. Those markers aren't mentioned here. The IHC is negative for S-100 and HMB-45, which helps rule out melanoma and nerve sheath tumors. So, with the given markers, GIST is less likely. 

The presence of α1-antichymotrypsin is interesting. That's a marker sometimes seen in histiocytic tumors or some sarcomas, but it's not specific. Vimentin is a mesenchymal marker, so supports sarcoma. The MDM2 and CDK4 positivity is key here. Well-differentiated and dedifferentiated liposarcomas typically have amplification of MDM2 and CDK4. However, those usually occur in the retroperitoneum or extremities. But the mesentery is part of the abdominal cavity, so possible. But the primary site here is the colon anastomosis. 

Another possibility is that this is a de novo sarcoma arising at the site of previous surgery. Post-surgical sarcomas are rare but possible. The original colon cancer was an adenocarcinoma, so a metachronous sarcoma could be a new primary. The spindle cell sarcoma with MDM2 and CDK4 positivity might fit into dedifferentiated liposarcoma. However, dedifferentiated liposarcoma usually has areas of well-differentiated liposarcoma, which might not be present here. Alternatively, another type of sarcoma with MDM2 amplification. 

Alternatively, considering the history of colon cancer, could this be a sarcomatoid carcinoma? But sarcomatoid carcinoma would typically have some epithelial markers, at least focal. Since AE1/AE3 is negative, that makes sarcomatoid carcinoma less likely. 

So, putting it all together: the tumor is a spindle cell sarcoma, undifferentiated type. The IHC shows MDM2 and CDK4 positivity, which suggests possible dedifferentiated liposarcoma. However, the final pathology report states undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma. Given that MDM2 and CDK4 are markers often associated with liposarcoma, but if the pathologist has ruled out liposarcoma (maybe no evidence of fat cells or well-differentiated component), then the diagnosis would be SCS. However, the presence of MDM2 and CDK4 might suggest a specific subtype. 

Wait, the final diagnosis provided in the case is undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma. But the question is to provide the final diagnosis based on the data. So perhaps the correct answer is spindle cell sarcoma, but considering the IHC, maybe dedifferentiated liposarcoma. But the final pathology result says SCS. Maybe the MDM2 and CDK4 are part of the features leading to the diagnosis. 

Alternatively, intimal sarcoma? But those are rare and usually in large vessels. 

Alternatively, considering the location at the anastomosis site and prior colon cancer, could this be a recurrence as a sarcoma? But colon adenocarcinoma recurrence typically would be carcinoma, unless there's sarcomatoid transformation. But again, AE1/AE3 negative. 

Alternatively, a myxoid sarcoma? The MRI suggested mucus accumulation. But the histology doesn't mention myxoid stroma. 

Given all this, the final diagnosis per the case is undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma (SCS) based on the pathology. But the presence of MDM2 and CDK4 might suggest a more specific type. However, since the case states the final pathology showed SCS, perhaps that's the answer. But maybe the MDM2 and CDK4 positivity is indicative of a dedifferentiated liposarcoma. Pathologists sometimes use MDM2 amplification (by FISH) to confirm dedifferentiated liposarcoma. If they did FISH and found MDM2 amplification, then dedifferentiated liposarcoma. But the case only mentions IHC, not FISH. So in this case, the diagnosis is undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma, but with MDM2 and CDK4 expression. However, per the provided data, the final diagnosis given is undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma. So the answer should align with that. But maybe the correct diagnosis here is dedifferentiated liposarcoma. Wait, the MRI suggested mucus accumulation. Myxoid liposarcoma has myxoid areas, but again, the markers here are MDM2 and CDK4. Myxoid liposarcoma is typically negative for MDM2. Dedifferentiated liposarcoma is MDM2 amplified. 

Hmm. The case says the final pathology showed undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma. But given the MDM2 and CDK4 positivity, the correct diagnosis might actually be dedifferentiated liposarcoma. However, sometimes MDM2 IHC can be positive in other sarcomas, but more specific for well-differentiated/dedifferentiated liposarcoma. Since the pathologist made the diagnosis of SCS, perhaps they considered that despite MDM2 positivity. But maybe that's a misdiagnosis. Alternatively, the case might have a typo. But the user is asking for the diagnosis based on the provided data. So the answer should be dedifferentiated liposarcoma or spindle cell sarcoma. 

Wait, the user included in the case that the final pathology showed undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma. Therefore, the answer would be that. But perhaps the MDM2 and CDK4 are pointing towards a specific subtype, but the pathologist's final call is SCS. So maybe the answer is spindle cell sarcoma. Alternatively, the correct diagnosis integrating the IHC is dedifferentiated liposarcoma. But the case says "final pathology showed undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma (SCS)." So perhaps that's the intended answer. However, in real practice, MDM2 and CDK4 positivity would prompt FISH for MDM2 amplification to confirm dedifferentiated liposarcoma. So if that's done and positive, then dedifferentiated liposarcoma. If not, then SCS. But the case doesn't mention FISH, only IHC. 

So, given all the information provided, the final answer is undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma. But maybe the presence of MDM2 and CDK4 is a red herring, or perhaps the pathologist considered other factors. So per the case, the answer is undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma (SCS).
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient has a history of ascending colon adenocarcinoma treated with resection. A new circumferential tumor at the anastomosis site was identified, with biopsy showing a malignant spindle cell tumor.  
<step 2> Immunohistochemistry (IHC) results are critical: positivity for MDM2 and CDK4, markers commonly associated with dedifferentiated liposarcoma, but negativity for epithelial markers (AE1/AE3) rules out carcinoma.  
<step 3> The absence of S-100 and HMB-45 excludes melanoma and nerve sheath tumors. The histology confirms atypical spindle cells in the muscularis propria, aligning with sarcoma.  
<step 4> Though MDM2/CDK4 positivity might suggest dedifferentiated liposarcoma, the final pathology explicitly states "undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma (SCS)," which is a diagnosis of exclusion after ruling out specific subtypes.  
<step 5> The location (anastomosis site) and prior surgery raise the possibility of a secondary sarcoma, but the pathologic classification takes precedence over imaging or historical context.  

### Answer:
Undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma (SCS)

reasoning_eval.step: The patient has a history of ascending colon adenocarcinoma treated with resection. A new circumferential tumor at the anastomosis site was identified, with biopsy showing a malignant spindle cell tumor.

Immunohistochemistry (IHC) results are critical: positivity for MDM2 and CDK4, markers commonly associated with dedifferentiated liposarcoma, but negativity for epithelial markers (AE1/AE3) rules out carcinoma.
The absence of S-100 and HMB-45 excludes melanoma and nerve sheath tumors. The histology confirms atypical spindle cells in the muscularis propria, aligning with sarcoma.

Though MDM2/CDK4 positivity might suggest dedifferentiated liposarcoma, the final pathology explicitly states "undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma (SCS)," which is a diagnosis of exclusion after ruling out specific subtypes.
The location (anastomosis site) and prior surgery raise the possibility of a secondary sarcoma, but the pathologic classification takes precedence over imaging or historical context.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Initial differential diagnosis includes recurrent carcinoma, gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST), leiomyosarcoma, synovial sarcoma, clear cell sarcoma, and liposarcoma at the anastomosis site.
Step 2: Sarcomatoid carcinoma is ruled out by the absence of carcinoma evidence and AE1/AE3 negativity.
Step 3: GIST and leiomyosarcoma are excluded based on histological differences and immunostaining results.
Step 4: Synovial sarcoma and clear cell sarcoma are excluded based on histology.
Step 5: Dedifferentiated liposarcoma (DLPS) is excluded due to the lack of well-differentiated liposarcoma components despite MDM2 and CDK4 positivity.
Step 6: Undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma is diagnosed based on morphology, immunostaining, and exclusion of other entities.
Final diagnosis: Undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma (SCS) at the anastomosis site following prior ileocecal resection for colorectal cancer.
